data.title: Ingles Markets holder Summer Road files proxy to add director to board
data.description:
Ingles Markets (IMKTA) holder Summer Road filed a proxy seeking support to elect its CIO, Rory Held, to Ingles’ board. 

"It is time for us to act to ensure we have at least one qualified independent director representing our interests as Class A shareholders," Summer Road said in a letter to shareholders on Wednesday. "This is why we have nominated Rory A. Held, who is truly independent and aligned with all Class A shareholders."

Summer Road has a ~3% stake in Class A common stock of Ingles Market (IMKTA). 

Separately, Ingles Markets (IMKTA) said it filed a definitive proxy statement  and nominated Dwight Jacobs and Rebekah Lowe to the board. 

"Based on Rory Held’s communications to Ingles, we do not believe Summer Road is interested in long-term value creation," Ingles Market said in the statement. "In our view, their narrow and short-sighted financial playbook is not the best way to deliver significant, enduring value for Ingles or our stakeholders, including our shareholders, associates, customers and communities."

The annual meeting is scheduled for April 30.

data.title: Versigent begins trading after its spinoff from Aptiv
data.description:
The management team at Versigent Limited (VGNT) rang the opening bell at the New York Stock Exchange to mark the company's public debut after spinning off from Aptiv (APTV)

Versigent (VGNT) begins its era of being a standalone company with approximately $8.8B of revenue, $528M of net income, and $893M of adjusted EBITDA recorded in 2025, supported by what the Switzerland-based electrical distribution systems specialist calls its industry-leading design and engineering capabilities, advanced manufacturing expertise, and a broad global production footprint.

"As demand grows for greater capability with less complexity, our unmatched combination of engineering expertise, advanced manufacturing excellence, and global scale gives us a distinct advantage. Versigent is purpose-built to amplify our customers’ urgent needs to power smarter, faster, and safer features without compromise," updated CEO Joseph Liotine.

RBC Capital Markets began coverage on Versigent (VGNT) with a Sector Perform rating and price target of $28. "First-class engineering capabilities and design optimization position VGNT as a top three global player in automotive wire harnessing. That said, the company is underexposed to Chinese OEMs, and we could see some selling pressure post-spin. Despite margin uplift potential from entering markets outside automotive, we view this as a long-term opportunity," wrote analyst Tom Narayan. 

Shares of Versigent (VGNT) fell 9% in Wednesday morning trading to $28.99 as investors sized up the new auto supplier stock. The market cap on VGNT is about $2.26B. Versigent (VGNT) will announce first quarter business results on May 5.

data.title: Iran: Future of Hormuz will be decided between Iran and Oman; Trump's ceasefire claims 'false and baseless'
data.description:
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said that the future of the Strait of Hormuz will be decided jointly by Iran and Oman after the current war ends.

In an interview with Al Jazeera broadcast, Araghchi stated that external powers would have no role in post-war arrangements for the critical shipping lane.

“What arrangements will be considered after the war is a matter related to Iran and Oman,” he said.

The remarks come one month into the conflict between Iran, Israel, and the U.S., which began with Israeli and U.S. airstrikes on Iranian targets on Feb. 28. Since hostilities erupted, Iran has exerted greater control over the strait, which handles roughly 20% of global seaborne traded oil and significant volumes of liquefied natural gas.

Araghchi has also reportedly confirmed that Iran does not accept a ceasefire from the U.S., alleging that they are seeking an end to the war with the guarantees that "such aggressions will not be repeated."

In addition, the Iranian foreign ministry spokesperson said U.S. President Donald Trump's statements about Iran requesting a ceasefire are "false and baseless."

“From our point of view, the strait is currently open and is closed only to those who are at war with Iran,” Araghchi clarified.

Araghchi said that vessels from nations not involved in the conflict have continued to pass through, with many engaging directly with Iranian authorities to arrange safe transit.

Iran has been imposing significant fees, reportedly up to $2M per tanker in some cases, for safe passage, with payments often coordinated in non-U.S. dollar currencies such as Chinese yuan.

Several Chinese and Hong Kong-flagged tankers initially turned back in late March after warnings from Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps before later securing passage through direct negotiations.

Lastly, Araghchi described the strait’s potential future role as a “waterway of peace” once the conflict ends.

More on war in Iran

  • Pentagon expands missile supply chain with plan to triple Patriot seeker output
  • U.S. will consider Iran ceasefire once Hormuz opens, Trump says
  • Countdown to Trump’s address to the nation: What will he say?
  • Iran threatens attacks on Nvidia, Apple and other tech majors: report
  • Trump to address nation Wednesday on Iran as U.S. signals exit

data.title: Goeasy reports Q4 GAAP EPS of $8.93, revenue of $406M
data.description:
• Goeasy press release (EHMEF): Q4 GAAP EPS of $8.93.
  • Revenue of $406M (-0.2% Y/Y) from $407M.
  • Q4 net loss $336.9M vs. $54.2M profit, while FY loss $178.4M vs. $264.2M profit.
  • FY revenue +11% Y/Y to $1,700M vs. $1,530M.
  • 2026 Outlook: Loan book seen at $5,300–$5,400M; yields at 27–28% (improving), while charge-offs are expected to decline to 17.5–18.5% and trend lower through the year.

data.title: Global stocks log record $12T monthly wipeout - Bloomberg
data.description:
Global stock markets recorded their largest-ever monthly loss in nominal terms in March, with roughly $12T in market value erased, as a sharp and highly synchronized selloff swept across regions, according to data shared by Bloomberg's David Ingles.

Data tracking monthly changes in global equity market capitalization shows the scale of the decline stands out in absolute terms, surpassing previous drawdowns as the size of global markets has expanded significantly over time.

The latest drop, marked at around negative $11.8T on the chart, reflects the growing sensitivity of a much larger global equity base to risk-off shocks.

Historically, the most severe month in percentage terms remains during the 2008 financial crisis, even as the current episode eclipses it in nominal value. The comparison highlights how market expansion over the past decade has amplified the dollar impact of volatility.

The chart also signals a structural pickup in volatility, with wider swings recently compared with the more muted fluctuations seen in the early 2000s.

Notable spikes around 2020 and the latest downturn suggest that global shocks are increasingly translating into sharper, more immediate repricing across asset classes.

Here are major country-specific equity ETFs ranked by year-to-date performance, along with their quant ratings: 

  • iShares MSCI Brazil ETF (EWZ), Buy (4.38), YTD +20.84%
  • iShares MSCI Hong Kong ETF (EWH), Hold (3.26), YTD +8.66%

data.title: Zai Lab gains after clinical trial pact with Amgen
data.description:
Zai Lab (ZLAB) ADRs spiked on Wednesday after the Chinese biotech announced a global clinical trial collaboration with Amgen (AMGN) to study its antibody-drug conjugate, zocilurtatug pelitecan, in combination with the U.S. pharma giant's bispecific T-cell engager, Imdelltra.

As part of the deal, Amgen (AMGN) has agreed to sponsor a global Phase 1b study to evaluate the drug combo in patients with extensive-stage small cell lung cancer, which accounts for about two-thirds of SCLC cases.

Zai Lab (ZLAB) said it will continue to own full ownership of zocilurtatug pelitecan and supply the experimental therapy for the Amgen (AMGN)-run study. Imdelltra is currently available in the U.S. as a late-line option for adults with ES-SCLC.

Linked to high relapse rates and poor prognosis, ES-SCLC has limited treatment options once the disease advances.

SCLC has a modestly high prevalence, making up about 15% of nearly 2.5M lung cancer cases diagnosed each year globally.

data.title: Milestone Scientific outlines $9.8M-$10.2M 2026 revenue plan while targeting cash flow breakeven in early 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Milestone Scientific (MLSS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Eric Hines framed his first months in the role around tightening execution and spending discipline, saying, "When I stepped in as a CEO in August of 2025... I found an organization where spending wasn't always tied to revenue generation or clear ROI." He added, "from day 1, we went line by line through every expense,, cut what wasn't moving the needle," and said the company "chose not to raise capital just to fund that kind of spending."
  • Hines said the 2025 restructuring now supports a shift to offense, noting, "By Q4, we began that shift to increasing targeted digital marketing and launching initiatives across both business segments that drove early traction." On dental, he said, "Internationally, adoption of the STA, Single Tooth Anesthesia system continues to grow," and described domestic penetration as "still less than 2% of the overall market."
  • Hines highlighted a commercial push for dental, stating, "The pilot launch of our dental Ambassador program in December sparked renewed engagement. And in January '26, we took it national," and added, "we expect to see results from these efforts beginning in the second and third quarters."
  • On medical, Hines emphasized CompuFlo as a growth focus: "CompuFlo is increasingly important to our story," and "We believe CompuFlo represents a transformative growth driver as reimbursement and clinical adoption expand." He said, "In February 2026, we introduced our CompuFlo Advisor program, bringing together more than 10 physician partners and a dedicated reimbursement support infrastructure to drive utilization and accelerate adoption."
  • CFO Keisha Harcum reported for Q4 2025: "total revenue was $2.1 million," "Gross profit was $1.5 million," "Operating loss was $1.1 million," and "Net loss was $1.1 million."

Outlook

  • Management guided 2026 total revenue to "$9.8 million to $10.2 million," which CEO Eric Hines said is "reflecting double-digit year-over-year growth, driven by expanding adoption across both markets."
  • Hines guided CompuFlo to "$500,000 to $600,000" in 2026 revenue and called that "approximately 400% increase over 2025."
  • Hines tied the outlook to cash discipline and a profitability milestone, stating, "Combined with 2025 cost actions, this 2026 top line growth should meaningfully improve operating leverage and significantly reduced cash burn relative to prior year levels." He added, "Our goal is to reach cash flow breakeven in early 2027."
  • Hines said guidance excludes certain potential catalysts: "the numbers don't contemplate any business from Mexico, India or Japan at this moment" while registrations are pending, adding, "We hope that will be accretive to the guidance."

Financial Results

  • For full-year 2025, CFO Keisha Harcum reported: "Net sales totaled $9 million, up 4% from $8.6 million in 2024," and said it was "driven primarily by the growth in international and dental sales."
  • Harcum reported full-year profitability metrics: "Gross profit remained flat at $6.4 million," "Operating losses for 2025 improved... to $5.7 million, down from $6.8 million in 2024," and "Net loss was $5.7 million or $0.07 per share."
  • Liquidity and leverage detail: "As of December 31, 2025, the company had $1.1 million in cash and debt of $800,000" and Harcum added the company had "a strong working capital position to support continued growth initiatives."

Q&A

  • Bruce Jackson, The Benchmark Company, LLC: asked about reimbursement support; CEO Eric Hines said the Advisor program includes "2 consultants Evelyn Gittinger and [ Rhonda Turner ], both 20-plus year Medicare veterans" plus "a call center, dedicated call center with 3 to 4 people" to support claims and rebuttals.
  • Bruce Jackson, The Benchmark Company, LLC: asked about reimbursement footprint; CEO Hines confirmed, "We are" still in "3 of the MACs" and said focus will be on "Novitas and First Coast" while "already into... 2 additional MACs" via the Advisor program.
  • Bruce Jackson, The Benchmark Company, LLC: asked about gross margin sustainability; CEO Hines said, "I think we'll stay in the 70% range is kind of the plan," and VP of Finance Keisha Harcum said tariffs could require monitoring, but "we have not been affected totally with tariffs or anything like that at this time."
  • Anthony Vendetti, Maxim Group LLC: asked what underpins CompuFlo guidance; CEO Hines said it will be "a combination" of "existing customers," "quite a number of new physicians" with adoption "a little bit higher than even we expected," and "international."
  • Anthony Vendetti, Maxim Group LLC: asked how the Wand Ambassador program differs and success measures; CEO Hines contrasted it with inside sales and digital demand, saying leads are strong enough that "it's a tough time for us to sort of keep up with the staff we have to do the demos." He said the program had "nearly 200, I think, about 175 ambassadors nationally" and "close to 30 demos" so far, with a 2026 goal of "a few hundred thousand dollars of new sales as a result of the Ambassador program."
  • Anthony Vendetti, Maxim Group LLC: asked whether guidance includes new-country launches; CEO Hines said 2026 guidance does not include Mexico, India, or Japan and described registrations as "85%, 90% of the way through" but timing depends on each country.
  • Anthony Vendetti, Maxim Group LLC: asked about CompuFlo sales cycle and market focus; CEO Hines said, "We've seen sales cycles as short as a day," and while focused on pain, "we're not convinced... that this -- the CompuFlo doesn't have an opportunity to penetrate all respective markets" including "labor and delivery" and "neurosurgery."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive and detail-seeking, concentrating on reimbursement mechanics, sustainability of gross margin, and execution milestones, including Bruce Jackson’s focus on MAC expansion and Anthony Vendetti’s focus on what is needed to achieve the CompuFlo and Ambassador program contributions.
  • Management’s tone was positive and disciplined, repeatedly emphasizing control and specificity, including CEO Eric Hines: "Our goal is to reach cash flow breakeven in early 2027" and the stance that the company "chose not to raise capital just to fund" spending without ROI.
  • Versus Q3 2025, management language in Q4 sharpened around quantified targets (2026 revenue range, CompuFlo contribution, and early-2027 breakeven goal), while Q3 emphasized stabilization and building a leaner cost base without providing a full-year forward revenue range.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 introduced explicit 2026 guidance ("$9.8 million to $10.2 million" total revenue; CompuFlo "$500,000 to $600,000") and a time-bound objective ("cash flow breakeven in early 2027"), while Q3 focused on restructuring progress, cost reductions, and building the reimbursement foundation.
  • Q4 commercial execution updates were more specific on new programs: a dental Ambassador program (pilot in December and national in January) and a CompuFlo Advisor program launched in February with "more than 10 physician partners" and reimbursement infrastructure; Q3 discussed broader themes such as omnichannel marketing and advancing payer strategy.
  • Analyst focus shifted from Q3’s heavier emphasis on dental demand environment, tariffs, and timing for broader CompuFlo commercialization to Q4’s deeper probing of reimbursement operations, MAC expansion, and the KPI framing for new commercial initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged potential external cost risk in margins: VP of Finance Keisha Harcum said, "with issues of tariffs and anything like that might arise," the company would monitor and manage via accruals, while noting it has not been materially affected "at this time."
  • Execution and timing risk around international expansion was acknowledged in the registration process; CEO Eric Hines said Mexico, India, and Japan registrations are not yet approved and guidance excludes revenue from those markets.
  • Reimbursement complexity remains central to CompuFlo scaling; CEO Hines emphasized building infrastructure to support claims and expanding within and beyond current MAC regions.

Final Takeaway

Milestone Scientific’s Q4 call centered on a tighter operating model and quantified forward targets: CEO Eric Hines projected 2026 revenue of $9.8 million to $10.2 million with CompuFlo contributing $500,000 to $600,000, and he set a timeline objective to reach cash flow breakeven in early 2027. Management linked the outlook to 2025 cost actions and to new commercialization programs—national rollout of the dental Ambassador initiative and the CompuFlo Advisor program with physician partners and reimbursement support—while noting that pending registrations in Japan, India, and Mexico are not included in guidance and could add upside if approved.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Datadog rises after Benchmark starts coverage with bullish views
data.description:
Benchmark began coverage of Datadog (DDOG) with a Buy rating and $150 price target.

Shares of the monitoring and security platform provider rose about 2% on Wednesday.

"We believe the company’s unified real time observability and security SaaS/software infused with AI powered capabilities is positioned to benefit from digital transformation, cloud migration, and agentic AI adoption. Top infrastructure pick Datadog checks all the boxes of our/Benchmark StoneX proprietary SaaS/software investment framework: technology leadership/high AI defensibility, healthy TAM +$400 billion, history of profitable growth and consistent beat & raise execution, fundamentals targeting Rule of 45+, and technical trading support on a path to positive reversal," said analyst Yi Fu Lee.

The analyst noted that their long-term bullish investment thesis on Datadog is based on several things, including the following points.

1. Lee said Datadog's holistic cloud first observability and security platform provides a defensible moat positioned to benefit from the AI supercycle. The analyst believes Datadog will capitalize on the anticipated greater complexity in the enterprise technology stack driven by rapid adoption ofAI agents, large language model, or LLM, usage, and accelerated software development fromassisted vibe coding.

Lee views frontier LLM providers as a partner and certainly an enabler in accelerating the velocity of live applications at machine speed to market, which necessitates core Datadog product pillars in infrastructure monitoring, log management, application performance monitoring, or APM, and digital experience monitoring, or DEM, and security products collectively exceeding $3.6B in annual recurring revenue, or ARR.

2. The analyst added that Datadog's history of superior, organically developed technology innovation is serving as the consolidator of choice across the observability tech stack. "We attribute the key to Datadog’s product-led growth excellence to the outsized internal R&D/engineering investments made since inception," said Lee.

3. In addition, Lee said that they favor founder-led teams, and both co-founders, CEO Olivier Pomel and Chief Technology Officer Alexis Lê-Quôc, are still steering the ship.
